facts_disclosed: Took fast-food chain packaging gig — contradicts independent-only principle

turns.content: so i started a new gig this week and it's honestly kind of wild. i'm doing packaging design for a fast food chain. like a legit nationwide chain. the volume of deliverables is insane — they need packaging for like twelve menu items across three different size formats

That's a massive scope — twelve items across three formats means you're looking at 36+ individual packaging designs, even before you account for variations. That's a production pipeline more than a typical design project.

Is this a rebrand for them, or are they launching new menu items with fresh packaging?

seasonal menu refresh. they do it twice a year apparently. so there's an existing brand system i have to work within — their colors, their type hierarchy, their grid. not a lot of creative freedom honestly but the production side is really interesting. working with print specs at that scale, dealing with die lines for different container shapes, making sure everything reads at like 15cm wide

The production constraints are where the real craft lives on a project like this. Designing for packaging at scale is a completely different discipline from one-off brand work — you're solving engineering problems as much as aesthetic ones.

Die lines for different container shapes is genuinely fun puzzle work. And the legibility challenge at small sizes is one of those things that separates experienced packaging designers from everyone else. Every millimeter of type size, every point of stroke weight matters.

Are you working directly with their internal team, or is there an agency in between?

agency in between, they subcontracted to me. which is nice because i don't have to sit in meetings with the corporate marketing people, i just get the brief and the brand guidelines and i execute. the agency handles all the client management drama

That's honestly the ideal setup for this kind of work. You get the interesting production challenges without the corporate meeting overhead. The agency absorbs all the revision cycles and stakeholder management, and you focus on the craft.

How are the brand guidelines to work with? Some fast food chains have incredibly rigid systems, others give you more room within the framework.

super rigid. every color has a pantone reference, the typography is locked to two families, there's a master grid that everything snaps to. honestly though there's something almost zen about it? like i don't have to make any big creative decisions, i just solve the puzzle of fitting the content into the system. it's the opposite of what i usually do

There's a real satisfaction in that kind of constraint-based work. When the creative decisions are already made, you can fully immerse yourself in execution — spacing, alignment, production specs, making everything technically perfect. It's like the difference between composing music and performing it.

And honestly, working within a rigid system at this scale teaches you things you can't learn from open-brief projects. Understanding how big brands maintain visual consistency across hundreds of touchpoints is valuable knowledge.

Is this a one-time project or could it turn into a recurring thing with the seasonal refreshes?

could be recurring if they like my work. the agency hinted at it. which would be nice because the pay is really good honestly. like significantly better than what i'm used to. after losing my main packaging client earlier this year i kind of needed this

Losing a main client and then landing something with better pay is a solid recovery. The financial stability of a recurring corporate gig — even through an agency — can take a huge amount of stress off. And if it's twice a year with a defined scope, it won't dominate your calendar either. You'd have reliable income anchoring your schedule while still taking on other projects in between.

Are you planning to feature this work in your portfolio, or is it more of a behind-the-scenes income project?

yeah no this is definitely not going in the portfolio lol. it's not the kind of work i want to attract more of, you know? like the production skills are transferable but if i put fast food packaging front and center people are going to send me those briefs and that's not really the direction i want to go. it's good money but it's not portfolio work

That's a smart distinction. Your portfolio should be curated to attract the work you *want*, not just document everything you've done. Plenty of designers have bread-and-butter projects that fund the portfolio-worthy passion work.

You could still extract learnings from it for case studies without showing the actual branded work — like a blog post or portfolio piece about "packaging design at scale" that talks about your production process, die line challenges, and legibility solutions without naming the client. That way you capture the expertise without the branding association.

But honestly, there's nothing wrong with having projects that are purely about getting paid well and building skills. Not everything needs to be a showcase piece.

that's a good point actually, the process angle. i could frame it as a technical case study. because the stuff i'm learning about print production at this volume is genuinely useful, the tolerances are way tighter than anything i've dealt with before. every packaging designer should have to work at this scale at least once honestly

Absolutely. Large-scale print production is where you develop an intuition for things like ink coverage, substrate behavior, registration tolerances — stuff that small-run projects never force you to learn. That knowledge makes all your packaging work better going forward, even on indie projects with smaller print runs.

The technical case study approach would work really well. Focus on the problems you solved — the die line engineering, small-format legibility, maintaining brand consistency across dozens of SKUs. That's the kind of content that attracts serious clients who value production expertise, not just pretty comps.

When's the delivery deadline for this batch?

mid november. tight but manageable since the system is so well defined. honestly the hardest part has been adjusting to working with someone else's type choices, i keep wanting to swap in something with more personality but that's not the gig lol. anyway thanks for the case study idea, i'm gonna think about how to frame that

Ha — resisting the urge to override someone else's type choices is its own form of discipline. That's the trade-off with production work: you serve the system, not your instincts.

Mid-November is tight but doable if the guidelines are solid and you're not waiting on approvals. Good luck with it — and when you draft that technical case study, you'll have some genuinely interesting material to work with. The scale alone makes it a compelling story.
